Default MDP and MDP Family are related as one is a single MDP representation while the other is a family of MDPs specified using environment parameters for traffic signal control.
The traffic signal control task is typically represented as a single MDP as shown in Figure 1a.

The traffic signal control task is underspecified and could easily be represented by a family of MDPs specified using environment parameters such as number of lanes, turn configurations, and traffic inflow levels as shown in Figure 1b.

Standard evaluation practice involves training and evaluating DRL methods on a point MDP or a subset of MDPs chosen from an implicit family of MDPs for assessing algorithmic generalization within traffic signal control task.

node_1: large families of mdps with limited computational budgets
edge: The challenge of dealing with large families of MDPs within limited computational budgets highlights the need for performance approximations and standardizations to manage the evaluation process effectively.

The family of MDPs has been formally modeled in multiple ways in the literature.

The most recent and general method is to model the problem as a Contextual Markov Decision Processes where all MDPs in the family share the same MDP configuration except for the transition function and the reward function.
In Hidden Parameter MDPs, only the transition function changes but keep the reward function fixed over the family of MDPs.
Epistemic POMDPs introduced by Ghosh et al. are a special case of a cMDP where the context is assumed to be unobservable.
They show that there is implicit partial observability under generalization to unseen test conditions from a limited number of training conditions.
This phenomenon translates even a fully observed MDP into POMDP.
In comparison to these formulations, we do not restrict what components of an MDP can or should change in evaluations. We let that be defined by the domain of the task.
